perturbation.original_text: 3.1.8 "Permitted Person" means – the Representatives of the Recipient; and any other person to whom the Recip ient discloses Confidential Information with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party;
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1.8 "Permitted Person" means – the Representatives of the Recipient;
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces an in-text contradiction by altering the definition of "Permitted Person" by removing the case where "any other person to whom the Recipient discloses Confidential Information with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party", creating uncertainty when other sections of the contract relies on the original definition.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.1 The Recipient shall be entitled to disclose the Confidential Information only to Permitted Persons, and then only to the extent that such disclosure is necessary for the Permitted Purpose and on a "need to know" basis.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the definition of 'Permitted Person' by removing the phrase 'any other person to whom the Recipient discloses Confidential Information with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party'. This directly contradicts section 5.1, which states 'The Recipient shall be entitled to disclose the Confidential Information only to Permitted Persons'. The original definition allowed disclosure to a broader group with consent, while the modified definition restricts it, creating a direct contradiction about who qualifies as a 'Permitted Person' for the purpose of confidential information disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: 19. DURATION The confidentiality undertaking as set out herein shall commence on the earliest of the first date on which any of the Parties released any Confidential Information to the other Party or the Ef fective Date and shall endure for a period of 10 (ten) years thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: 19. DURATION The confidentiality undertaking as set out herein shall commence on the earliest of the first date on which any of the Parties released any Confidential Information to the other Party or the Ef fective Date and shall endure perpetually.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces an in-text contradiction by altering the duration in which the confidentiality undertaking will take effect, creating uncertainty when other sections of the contract relies on the original duration.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 25. CONTINUING EFFECTIVENESS OF CERTAIN PROVISIONS The expiration or termination of this Agreement shall not affect such of the provisions of this Agreement as expressly provide that they will operate after any such expiration or termination or which of necessity must continue to have effect after such expiration or termination, notwiths tanding that the clauses themselves do not expressly provide for this.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a confidentiality undertaking lasting 10 years, while the changed text makes it perpetual. Section 25 concerns provisions that continue after the 'expiration or termination' of the agreement. If the confidentiality agreement is now perpetual (as stated in the changed text), then it technically never expires or terminates, thus rendering Section 25 potentially inapplicable to the confidentiality undertaking, which contradicts its general intention regarding post-termination effects. This is a clear and direct conflict in the duration of the confidentiality agreement, leading to uncertainty and potential unenforceability, which aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 20. DISPUTE RESOLUTION 20.1 A Party that is of the view that a Dispute has arisen shall give written notice thereof to the other Parties, provided that such notice shall clearly identify the D ispute and provide full particularity thereof, and the Parties shall thereafter take immediate steps to attempt to resolve the Dispute through their senior executives or other representatives who have the necessary authority to make binding decisions with respect to such Dispute (Senior Executives).
perturbation.changed_text: 20. DISPUTE RESOLUTION 20.1 A Party that is of the view that a Dispute has arisen shall give written notice thereof to the other Parties, provided that such notice shall broadly identify the D ispute and provide general detail thereof, and the Parties shall thereafter take steps to attempt to resolve the Dispute through their junior executives or other representatives who have no authority to make binding decisions with respect to such Dispute.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces an in-text contradiction by altering the procedure of dispute resolution in how a notice must be made, and what kind of executives must be responsible for resolving the disputes, creating uncertainty when other sections of the contract relies on the original process.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 20.2 If the Senior Executives cannot agree on a resolution of the Dispute within 15 Business Days of receipt of the notice referred to in clause 19.1, then the Dispute may be referred within a further period of 20 B usiness Days (Arbitration Initiating Period) to arbitration in accordance with this clause 19 unless the Parties agree in writing prior to the expiry of the Arbitration Initiating Period to refer such Dispute for expert determination in accordance with thi s clause 19.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the original text by saying that the notice should 'broadly identify' the dispute and provide 'general detail,' while the original text mandated that the notice 'clearly identify' the dispute and 'provide full particularity thereof.' This is a contradiction. Furthermore, the change specifies 'junior executives' with 'no authority to make binding decisions,' contradicting the original text's requirement for 'senior executives' with 'the necessary authority to make binding decisions.' The contradicted text, clause 20.2, relies on the decisions of the 'Senior Executives.' By removing the authority of the executives, there is a strong contradiction.
